coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent enzyme-linked immunoassay strategy based on enzyme-triggered in-situ synthesis of fluorescent copper nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ruiying
Liu, Qiang
Jin, Yan
Li, Baoxin
coredata.dc:description: The routine enzyme-linked immunesorbent assay (ELISA) with colorimetric readout is unsuitable for detecting low concentrations of target molecules. Herein, we proposed one sensitive fluorescence immunoassay strategy using alkaline phosphatase (ALP) as the labeling enzyme and copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) as fluorescent indicator. In this strategy, ALP specifically catalyzes the hydrolysis of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) to yield ascorbic acid, and then the resultant ascorbic acid interacts with Cu2+ in the presence of DNA template to in-situ synthesize the fluorescent DNA-templated CuNCs. The corresponding fluorescence signal is related to the amount of ALP conjugated on antibody, and the fluorescence intensity can be used to detect target antigen through an ELISA platform. Human immunoglobulin G (IgG) is taken as the model antigen for the proof-of-concept, and human IgG could be detected in 0.05 − 12 ng/mL range. The detection limit was 7 pg/mL, which is much lower than those in most of the previously reported ELISA. This simple turn-on fluorescent ELISA platform provides a cost-effective approach for sensitive detection of target proteins.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Immunoassay
Alkaline phosphatase
Copper nanoclusters
Fluorescence assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of the fluorescence strategy for ALP detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing the fluorescent turn-on biosensor for ALP based on ALP-triggered in-situ synthesis of fluorescent CuNCs.
Schematic illustration of fluorescence ELISA strategy through ALP-triggered in-situ synthesis of dsDNA-templated CuNCs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the Cu2+-DNA reaction system at different conditions: (a) the addition of H2O, (b) the addition of ascorbate (2.5 mM), (c) the addition of AAP (2.5 mM), (d) the addition of AAP (2.5 mM) and ALP (20 U/L).
TEM image of the as-prepared CuNCs and its size distribution histogram.
Effects of sequence length of poly(AT-TA) dsDNA (A) and concentration of AT-15 dsDNA (B) on the system response.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the system upon addition of ALP standards with various activities (from bottom to top: 0, 0.04, 0.05, 0.08, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0, 10, 20, 50, 100 U/L).

Fluorescence responses of the developed method against ALP or other control enzymes/proteins and ions. The concentration of ALP was 1 U/L, the level of other enzymes was 10 U/L or 10 μg/mL, and the concentrations of mental ions were 10 mM.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of this sensing system toward IgG with different concentrations (from bottom to top: 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 10, 12, 20, 50, 100, 200 ng/mL). (B) Fluorescence intensities at 575 nm of the system vs IgG concentration (insert: linear calibration plots for IgG concentration).

head.ce:title: Fluorescent enzyme-linked immunoassay strategy based on enzyme-triggered in-situ synthesis of fluorescent copper nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science of Shaanxi Province, School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi’an, 710062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science of Shaanxi Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Used dsDNA-templated copper nanoclusters as signal indicators, a simple, facile and sensitive fluorescence immunoassay was suggested.

The routine enzyme-linked immunesorbent assay (ELISA) with colorimetric readout is unsuitable for detecting low concentrations of target molecules. Herein, we proposed one sensitive fluorescence immunoassay strategy using alkaline phosphatase (ALP) as the labeling enzyme and copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) as fluorescent indicator. In this strategy, ALP specifically catalyzes the hydrolysis of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) to yield ascorbic acid, and then the resultant ascorbic acid interacts with Cu2+ in the presence of DNA template to in-situ synthesize the fluorescent DNA-templated CuNCs. The corresponding fluorescence signal is related to the amount of ALP conjugated on antibody, and the fluorescence intensity can be used to detect target antigen through an ELISA platform. Human immunoglobulin G (IgG) is taken as the model antigen for the proof-of-concept, and human IgG could be detected in 0.05 − 12 ng/mL range. The detection limit was 7 pg/mL, which is much lower than those in most of the previously reported ELISA. This simple turn-on fluorescent ELISA platform provides a cost-effective approach for sensitive detection of target proteins.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzyme-linked immunesorbent assay (ELISA), which was pioneered by Engvall and Perlman [1] and van Weeman and Schuurs [2] in 1971, makes the best of the highly specific recognition of antibody-antigen and the efficiently catalytic amplification of an enzyme. At present, ELISA is the most widely used mean of immunoassay [3,4], and has been extensively used in clinical diagnosis, food safety, environmental protection, laboratory research, and home-health care [3–11]. Conventional ELISA usually employs alkaline phosphatase (ALP) or horseradish peroxidase (HRP) as the labeling enzyme, and HRP or ALP catalyzes the transform of the substrate into the colored product. Color change can be monitored with a simple spectrophotometer and even naked-eyes. The colorimetric signal output is simple and low-cost, but this readout can only be used at high concentration of the target molecule, therefore making colorimetric ELISA unsuitable for detecting ultralow concentrations of species [12]. Up to date, a large of ELISA methods based on alternative signal-transduce principles have been developed to overcoming the shortcomings of conventional ELISA, including fluorescence [13–15], chemilumiescence [16,17], electrochemistry [12,18,19], photoelectron-chemical method [3,20], surface enhancement Raman scattering [4] and plasmonic method [21–23].

With advances in nanoscience and nanotechnology, some nanomaterial-based signal readout strategies have been extended into immunoassays [11]. Various nanomaterials, such as gold nanoparticles [24,25], gold nanorods [26,27], gold nanobipyramids [28], silver nanoparticles [29], silicon nanoparticles [14,30], graphene oxide [31], carbon dots [32] and quantum dots [33], are used to enhance the sensitivity of immunoassays [11]. In most of the nanomaterials-based immunoassays, the nanomaterials are typically used as the labels to replace the labeling enzyme (HRP or ALP) of the conventional ELISA. So, the nanomaterials-based immunoassays cannot directly utilize the conventional ELISA platform (including commercially available ALP-labeled (or HRP-labeled) antibodies). On other hands, the synthesis of nanomaterials-antibody conjugates is unavoidably complicated, time consuming and laborious. These shortcomings hamper the wide application of the nanomaterials-based immunoassays in practice. Thus, exploring more suitable nanomarticle-based immunoassay strategy, which is simple and can directly make use of commercially available antibody-HRP (or ALP) conjugates as well as the conventional ELISA platform, is still desirable and significant.

As a promising kind of fluorescent nanomaterials, DNA-templated copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) have recently attracted more and more attention [34,35]. DNA-templated CuNCs possess many excellent properties. For example, preparation of the DNA-templated CuNCs is very fast and much convenient (about 10 min in aqueous solution at ambient temperature and pressure) [35,36]. As the precursors for synthesis of CuNCs, copper and ascorbic acid are not only abundant, easily available and low-cost, but also are essential and important micronutrients for all organisms. At the same time, DNA molecule is non-toxic with good biocompatibility. So, compared with other heavy-metal nanoparticles (such as PbSe and CdTe quantum dots), the DNA-templated CuNCs are more environment-friendly and safer. Furthermore, DNA-templated CuNCs show strong fluorescent emission at 570 − 600 nm with the excitation at 340 nm. The Stokes shift of the CuNCs’ fluorescence is rather big (∼240 nm), which is very favorable for eliminating the interference from background signal in the complex biological systems [35–37]. The intriguing properties make DNA-stabilized CuNCs as attractive fluorescence nano-dyes for signal transducing and outputting. However, use of DNA-templated CuNCs in bio-analysis is still in its early stages [35]. Therefore, it is very important and encouraging to exploit it in further application.

Herein, we presented a new fluorescence ELISA strategy via ALP-triggered in-situ synthesis of DNA-templated CuNCs. In this strategy (Scheme 1 ), ALP can remove the phosphate group from ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP), thus yielding ascorbate [6,7,14]. Then resultant ascorbic acid interacts with Cu2+ in the presence of DNA template to produce the fluorescent DNA-templated CuNCs. Furthermore, considering that ALP is one of the most used enzyme labels in the routine ELISA with colorimetric readout, we have extended this fluorescent enzyme assay into the ALP-labeled immunoassay. Human immunoglobulin G (IgG), which is the most common type of antigen found in circulation, was taken as the model target antigen for the proof-of-concept. This assay directly utilized the routine ELISA platform, which included a commercialized polystyrene microtitier plate and the commercially available ALP-conjugated secondary antibody. Therefore, this strategy introduced the CuNCs-based fluorescent indicator into the ALP-based routine ELISA to present a facile and sensitive fluorescent immunoassay platform.

Bovine serum albumin (BSA), l-ascorbic acid and l-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). CuSO4 was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Human immunoglobulin G (IgG), rabbit anti-IgG, ALP-conjugated antibody were obtained from Sangon Biological Engineering Technology and Services Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). ELISA kits for IgG were bought from Suzhou Biocalvin Co., Ltd. (Suzhou, China). All oligonucleotides used in this study were commercially synthesized and purified by Sangon Biological Engineering Technology & Services Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) stock solutions were prepared by using equimolar complete complementary single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) in pH 7.6 3-(N-morpholine)opropanesulfonic acid (MOPS) buffer (10 mM MOPS and 150 mM NaCl). The ssDNA was heated to 90 °C for 10 min, and cooled slowly down to room temperature (ca. 20 °C), and then the dsDNA formed. Before use, the dsDNA solution was diluted to the desired concentrations with the same MOPS buffer. All other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade. These serum samples were gifted from Shaanxi Normal University Hospital. The used ultrapure water in whole experiments was obtained from a Millipore Milli Q water purification system (Billerica, MA).

One F-4600 Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was used to measure fluorescence spectra. Ultraviolet spectra were performed on a U-3900H UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). All pH measurements were made with Thermo Scientific Orion Star A111 desktop pH meter. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was measured using a Tecnai G2 F20 electron microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV with a CCD camera.

ALP assay based on the turn-on fluorescence was performed using the following procedure. 20 μL of 2.5 mM AAP and 20 μL of ALP with various concentrations incubated at 37 °C for 60 min. After that, 20 μL MOPS buffer (pH 7.6, 10 mM MOPS, 150 mM NaCl), 20 μL dsDNA (1 μM) and 20 μL CuSO4 (0.5 mM) were subsequently added for another incubation at room temperature for about 5 min to form the DNA-templated CuNCs. At last, the fluorescent spectrum of the resultant solution was recorded in the 520–650 nm range with the excitation at 340 nm. The fluorescent intensity at 575 nm could be used to quantify ALP activity.

Before the fluorescence immunoassay, a functionalized procedure was performed to immobilize the capture antibody (primary antibody) on one 96-well plates. Briefly, 100 μL of 25 μg/mL anti-human IgG antibody in pH 7.4 10 mM phosphate buffer saline (PBS) was added into one well of a 96-well polystyrene plate and incubated at 4 °C overnight. After discarding the solution, the 96-well polystyrene plate was washed with 250 μL of 0.05% Tween-20-PBS (pH 7.4) for three times. Then, 300 μL of 5% BSA was injected into each well and incubated at 37 °C for 60 min in order to block nonspecific binding sites on the well. After washing again, 100 μL human IgG with various concentrations were added into each well and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C, following with washing steps. Next, 100 μL ALP-conjugated secondary antibody (1:500) was added into the well. After incubation for 60 min at 37 °C, 40 μL AAP (2.5 mM) was injected into each well for an enzyme reaction for 60 min at ca. 37 °C. Afterward, 80 μL pH 7.6 MOPS buffer (10 mM MOPS and 150 mM NaCl), 40 μL dsDNA (1 μM) and 40 μL CuSO4 (0.5 mM) solutions were subsequently added into the well for another incubation at room temperature (ca. 25 °C) for 5 min to form the DNA-templated CuNCs. At last, the fluorescence intensities of the resultant CuNCs solutions were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the fluorescence strategy for ALP detection
ce:section.ce:para: ALP can catalyze the hydrolysis of various phosphate esters to yield their corresponding alcohols and phosphate. AAP is a commercially available substrate of ALP, and ALP catalyze the hydrolysis of AAP to produce ascorbate [6,7,14,38]. On the other hand, in the presence of dsDNA, ascorbate reduces Cu(II) to Cu(I) at ambient temperature and pressure, and then the Cu(I) disproportionation can form CuNCs on dsDNA template [34–36]. The formed dsDNA-templated CuNCs emit strong fluorescence. Furthermore, among many reducing agents (such as citrate sodium, NaBH4, NH2OH, N2H4·H2O and ascorbic acid), only ascorbic acid could reduce Cu2+ into fluorescent CuNCs with DNA as template [38]. As presented in Scheme 1, combing the ALP-catalyzed hydrolysis reaction with the synthesis reaction of fluorescent CuNCs, we proposed one turn-on fluorescent sensing strategy for ALP activity.

In order to validate the feasibility of this CuNCs-based fluorescence strategy for ALP assay, some experiments were carried out. As shown in Fig. 1 , the fluorescent intensity of this Cu2+−dsDNA system in the presence of AAP alone (curve b) is almost zero and the same as the background noise (curve a), which reveals that AAP (ascorbic acid-phosphate) by itself could not reduce Cu(II) to form fluorescent CuNCs. ALP alone could not also reduce Cu(II) to form fluorescent CuNCs. By contrast, simultaneous existence of ALP and AAP in the Cu2+−DNA system can induce generation of fluorescent CuNCs (curve b). This reason is that the ALP-catalyzed hydrolysis reaction of AAP produces ascorbate, and the subsequent ascorbate can react with Cu2+−DNA to generate fluorescent CuNCs. This indicated strongly that ALP can trigger the dephosphorylation of AAP, producing fluorescent CuNCs from the Cu2+−DNA system. Furthermore, in order to confirm the formation of CuNCs in the above process, we measured the TEM images of the final reaction solution. It can be seen from Fig. 2 that most of the CuNCs were spherical, and the degree of agglomeration was lower. The generated CuNCs was distributed in 1.7 − 4.7 nm range with an average particle size of 3.2 nm (inset of Fig. 2). In short, the above experimental results showed that this CuNCs-based fluorescent strategy could be applied potentially to detect ALP.

In order to obtain better sensing performance, the experiment conditions of ALP assay were optimized. In this sensing strategy, DNA-templated CuNCs are acted as the fluorescent indicator. The fluorescent intensity of DNA-templated CuNCs highly depend on sequence and concentration of DNA template [39], and the poly(AT-TA) dsDNA is more effective template for preparation of highly fluorescent CuNCs [39]. We designed the poly(AT-TA) dsDNA sequences with different lengths to act as templates for synthesis of CuNCs under the low concentration of ascorbate. The experimental results (Fig. 3 A) showed that the poly(AT-TA) dsDNA with sequence length of 15 bp (AT-15 dsDNA) was the best template for fluorescent CuNCs, whereas shorter or longer AT sequences couldn’t valid induced formation of CuNCs at a low concentration of ascorbate. The effect of template concentration was also investigated (Fig. 3B), and the highest signal-to-noise ratio was achieved when using 1 μM AT-15 dsDNA as the template at the constant concentrations of Cu2+ and ascorbate. On the other hand, we investigated also the effect of the enzymatic reaction condition on the fluorescence response, and the experimental results (Figure S1) showed that the optimal AAP concentration, reaction temperature, media pH and reaction time were 2.5 mM, 37 °C, pH 8.5, and 60 min, respectively.

Under the optimized conditions, we assessed the performance for this turn-on fluorescent ALP assay. The fluorescent spectra of the Cu2+-DNA system were recorded after the addition of AAP pre-treated with various activities of ALP in 0.04 − 100 U/L range. Fig. 4 illustrated that the fluorescent intensity of this biosensing system increased gradually with increasing ALP activity. There was good linear relationship between the fluorescent intensity (F) at 575 nm and ALP activity (C, U/L) in the range of 0.04 U/L to 8 U/L (Figure S2), and the fitted linear equation could be described as F = 230.8 C + 1.956, R 2 = 0.9947. The limit of detection in this ALP assay was estimated to be 0.006 U/L (3σ), which was much lower than that of these recently reported assays [13,14,28,34,38]. Furthermore, the specificity of this sensing platform for ALP activity was also studied using some possible interferences, including K+, Mg2+, Fe3+, bovine serum albumin (BSA), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and lysozyme (Lys). The results (Fig. 5 ) that none of them induced an obvious fluorescence response in this CuNPs-based sensing system. High sensitivity and high specificity would make this system an ideal sensing for ALP activity.

ALP is one of most widely used enzymatic labels in ELISA. This highly sensitive and selective method for ALP assay has a great potential to be expanded to the immunoassay by the aid of commercial ALP-labeled antibodies. As shown in Scheme 2 , we took IgG as the model antigen to evaluate the performance of CuNCs-based ELISA platform for proof-of-concept. With adding more human IgG into the 96-well polystyrene plate, more ALP-antibody conjugates should be immobilized. On the other hand, the amount of ALP-antibody conjugates directly involves with the yielded ascorbate, and is indirectly related to the synthesis of fluorescent CuNCs. Thus, such response processes result in the antigen-dependent change on the fluorescent intensity of the resultant solution. Under the above optimized experimental conditions, the experiments were carried out through adding human IgG with various concentrations into the system to study whether the fluorescent change could be used to detect IgG. Fig. 6 A gives the fluorescent response of this system to human IgG with different concentrations. It can be seen that the fluorescent intensity increased with gradually increasing human IgG concentration from 0 to 200 ng/mL. As showed in Fig. 6B, there is a good linear relationship between the fluorescent intensities at 575 nm and human IgG concentration in the range of 0.05 − 12 ng/mL. The fitted linear equation is described as F = 96.4C + 2.67, R 2 = 0.997 (where F is the fluorescent intensity at 575 nm, C is IgG concentration (ng/mL), and R is a correlation coefficient). The limit of detection was estimated to be 7 pg/mL (3σ), which is much lower than that in the recently reported immunoassays for human IgG [27,38,40]. We choose 0.1 ng/mL, 1 ng/ml and 10 ng/ml human IgG to check the repeatability of this method. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 5% for 7 measurements for the same concentration of IgG. Furthermore, we also investigated the selectivity of the proposed immunosensor. Carcino-embryonic antigen (CEA), porcine serum albumin (PSA), cancer antigen (CA) 127, CA153 and CA199 were selected as the competitors. The results (Figure S3) showed that other proteins caused no obvious fluorescence emission. The control experiments showed that these proteins also did not affect the intensity of DNA-templated CuNCs. Therefore, this “turn-on” fluorescence immunoassay was sensitive and selective.

In order to demonstrate the reliability of this proposed ELISA platform in clinical application, the serums from three healthy donors were assayed with this fluorescence immunoassay. Meanwhile, the same samples were also analyzed by a commercial ELISA kit. Table S1 described the results obtained by this proposed ELISA and commercial ELISA for the detection of human IgG in the samples. The results indicated that there was no significant difference between the two methods. This suggests the great potential of this CuNCs-based fluorescence immunoassay as an alternative to the commercial ELISA for human IgG detection. Of course, this CuNCs-based fluorescence immunoassay can be easily extended to determine other targets with the aid of their corresponding antibodies.

In summary, combing the ALP-enabled hydrolysis of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate to ascorbate with the ascorbate-controlled synthesis of fluorescent CuNCs, a sensitive and turn-on fluorescence assay was successfully designed for detection of ALP. Furthermore, taking ALP as the labelling enzyme and CuNCs as the signal probes, one turn-on fluorescent ELISA is thereby developed. The designed ELISA platform showed high sensitivity, and the detection limit was 7 pg/mL for human IgG (model antigen). The assay can directly use the commercialized polystyrene microtitier plate and the commercially ALP-conjugated secondary antibody. Thus, this protocol can easily applied in practice. On the other hand, the assay has the advantages of CuNCs-based fluorescent indicator, such as facile synthesis and low-cost. In view of the above advantages, this new strategy might hold a great potential for further applications in biomedical research and clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775099 and 21475083).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.09.128.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

Paper-based chemiluminescence immunodevice with temporal controls of reagent transport technique
Sandwich-type electrochemical immunoassay based on Co3O4@MnO2-thionine and pseudo-ELISA method toward sensitive detection of alpha fetoprotein
Dopamine-loaded liposomes for in-situ amplified photoelectrochemical immunoassay of AFB1 to enhance photocurrent of Mn2+-doped Zn3(OH)2V2O7nanobelts
Plasmonic ELISA for the ultrasensitive detection of disease biomarkers with the naked eye
Plasmonic ELISA for the detection of analytes at ultralow concentrations with the naked eye

Copper-mediated amplification allows readout of immunoassays by the naked eye
Versatile and amplified biosensing through enzymatic cascade reaction by coupling alkaline phosphatase in situ generation of photoresponsive nanozyme
Peroxidase-like activity of ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid and its application for ultrasensitive detection of tumor biomarkers and circular tumor cells
High-resolution colorimetric assay for rapid visual readout of phosphatase activity based on gold/silver core/shell nanorod
Highly uniform gold nanobipyramids for ultrasensitive colorimetric detection of influenza virus
Optical transformation of a CdTe quantum dot-based paper sensor for a visual fluorescence immunoassay induced by dissolved silver ions
Fluorescent silicon nanoparticles-based ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for sensitive detection of ethyl carbamate in red wine
Improvement of enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay for the multicolor detection of biomarkers

Molecular-counting-free and electrochemiluminescent single-molecule immunoassay with dual-stabilizers-capped CdSe nanocrystals as labels

A fluorescent ELISA based on the enzyme-triggered synthesis of poly (thymine)-templated copper nanoparticles
Sequence dependent dsDNA-templated formation of fluorescent copper nanoparticles
The chemical redox modulated switch-on fluorescence of carbon dots for probing alkaline phosphatase and its application in an immunoassay

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ruiying Li is a graduate student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University. Her current research is focused on development of novel immunoassay.

Qiang Liu is a Ph.D candidate in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University. His current research is focused on development of fluorescence and chemiluminescence biosensing.
Yan Jin is a full professor in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University. Her current research is focused on fluorescence biosensing.
Baoxin Li is a full professor in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Normal University. His current research is focused on molecular luminescence and bioanalytical chemistry.
